32	# a process spawn per prompt with an in-memory render. Preserve the fds when
33	# the script is re-sourced mid-session so the running daemon is reused.
34	_omp_serve_fd_in=${_omp_serve_fd_in:--1}
35	_omp_serve_fd_out=${_omp_serve_fd_out:--1}
36	_omp_serve_pid=${_omp_serve_pid:-0}

43	function _omp_set_cursor_position() {
44	  # not supported in Midnight Commander
45	  # see https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/3415
46	  if [[ $_omp_cursor_positioning == 0 ]] || [[ -v MC_SID ]]; then
47	    return
48	  fi
49	
50	  local oldstty=$(stty -g)
51	  stty raw -echo min 0
52	
53	  local pos
54	  echo -en '\033[6n' >/dev/tty
55	  read -r -d R pos
56	  pos=${pos:2} # strip off the esc-[
57	  local parts=(${(s:;:)pos})
58	
59	  stty $oldstty
60	
61	  export POSH_CURSOR_LINE=${parts[1]}
62	  export POSH_CURSOR_COLUMN=${parts[2]}

65	# template function for context loading
66	function set_poshcontext() {
67	  return
68	}
69	
70	# cleanup stream resources
71	function _omp_cleanup_stream() {
72	  # unregister handler first (prevents handler firing on closed fd)
73	  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_stream_fd 2>/dev/null
74	  # close fd — process gets SIGPIPE and terminates
75	  [[ $_omp_stream_fd -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_stream_fd}<&-" 2>/dev/null
76	  _omp_stream_fd=-1
77	}
78	
79	# start oh-my-posh stream process, block until first prompt arrives
80	function _omp_start_streaming() {
81	  # cleanup any stale streams
82	  _omp_cleanup_stream
83	
84	  # the transient prompt for this cycle streams in alongside the primary
85	  # prompt updates, invalidate the previous cycle's version
86	  _omp_transient_prompt=""
87	
88	  # build command with all context
89	  local -a stream_cmd=(
90	    "$_omp_executable" stream
91	    --save-cache
92	    --shell=zsh
93	    --shell-version="$ZSH_VERSION"
94	    --status=$_omp_status
95	    --pipestatus="${_omp_pipestatus[*]}"
96	    --no-status=$_omp_no_status
97	    --execution-time=$_omp_execution_time
98	    --job-count=$_omp_job_count
99	    --stack-count=$_omp_stack_count
100	    --terminal-width="${COLUMNS:-0}"
101	  )
102	
103	  # start process substitution — no PID tracking needed
104	  # closing fd sends SIGPIPE which terminates oh-my-posh
105	  # redirect stream process stdin to prevent it from interfering with command output
106	  exec {_omp_stream_fd}< <(exec "${stream_cmd[@]}" </dev/null 2>/dev/null)
107	
108	  if [[ $_omp_stream_fd -lt 0 ]]; then
109	    return 1
110	  fi
111	
112	  # block until first prompt arrives (mirrors PowerShell's polling loop)
113	  IFS= read -r -u $_omp_stream_fd -d $'\0' _omp_primary_prompt
114	  if [[ $? -ne 0 || -z "$_omp_primary_prompt" ]]; then
115	    _omp_cleanup_stream
116	    return 1
117	  fi
118	
119	  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"
120	
121	  return 0
122	}
123	
124	# async handler: called when data available on fd (reads single value)
125	function _omp_async_handler() {
126	  local fd=$1
127	  local record
128	
129	  # read single null-delimited record (stream emits one value per \0)
130	  IFS= read -r -u $fd -d $'\0' record
131	  if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
132	    if [[ -z "$record" ]]; then
133	      # EOF — only clean up if this is still the active stream fd.
134	      # A stale handler from a previous prompt cycle must not close the current stream.
135	      [[ $_omp_stream_fd -eq $fd ]] && _omp_cleanup_stream
136	      return 0
137	    fi
138	  fi
139	
140	  # a record prefixed with U+001E carries the transient prompt: cache it for
141	  # the line-init widget so rendering the transient prompt needs no CLI call
142	  if [[ $record == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
143	    _omp_transient_prompt=${record#$'\x1e'}
144	    return 0
145	  fi
146	
147	  _omp_primary_prompt=$record
148	  PS1="$_omp_primary_prompt"
149	  zle reset-prompt 2>/dev/null
150	
151	  return 0
152	}

154	# === serve daemon ===
155	
156	function _omp_serve_stop() {
157	  # unregister the zle watcher before closing its fd
158	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out 2>/dev/null
159	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&-" 2>/dev/null
160	  [[ $_omp_serve_fd_out -ge 0 ]] && eval "exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&-" 2>/dev/null
161	  _omp_serve_fd_in=-1
162	  _omp_serve_fd_out=-1
163	}
164	
165	function _omp_serve_start() {
166	  _omp_serve_stop
167	
168	  # The daemon's stderr must never reach the terminal (a Go panic would
169	  # corrupt the display).
170	  coproc "$_omp_executable" serve --shell=zsh 2>/dev/null
171	  [[ $? -ne 0 ]] && return 1
172	  _omp_serve_pid=$!
173	
174	  # Duplicate both directions to session fds: the duplicates survive a later
175	  # `coproc` (ours or the user's) replacing the coproc slot. The stderr
176	  # redirect (suppressing "no coprocess" when the daemon failed to start) must
177	  # be scoped to a block: on a redirection-only `exec`, zsh applies every
178	  # listed redirection to the shell permanently, which would silence the
179	  # session's stderr for good.
180	  { exec {_omp_serve_fd_out}<&p {_omp_serve_fd_in}>&p } 2>/dev/null
181	  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 || $_omp_serve_fd_out -lt 0 ]]; then
182	    _omp_serve_stop
183	    return 1
184	  fi
185	
186	  # Keep the daemon out of the job table: it must not show up in `jobs` or
187	  # inflate the job-count segment. Lifetime is governed by the fds - closing
188	  # them (or the shell dying) EOFs the daemon's stdin and it exits.
189	  disown %+ 2>/dev/null
190	
191	  # One session-long watcher delivers async records (segment updates and the
192	  # transient refresh) while zle is active. It never races the synchronous
193	  # read in _omp_serve_render: zle - and therefore this watcher - is not
194	  # active while precmd runs.
195	  zle -F $_omp_serve_fd_out _omp_serve_async_handler 2>/dev/null
196	
197	  return 0
198	}

200	function _omp_serve_escape() {
201	  # JSON string escaping using native parameter expansion; the result is
202	  # returned in REPLY. Any control characters left after the named escapes
203	  # are stripped - JSON forbids them raw.
204	  local s=$1
205	  s=${s//\\/\\\\}
206	  s=${s//\"/\\\"}
207	  s=${s//$'\n'/\\n}
208	  s=${s//$'\r'/\\r}
209	  s=${s//$'\t'/\\t}
210	  REPLY=${s//[[:cntrl:]]/}

213	function _omp_serve_request() {
214	  # A write to a dead daemon's pipe raises SIGPIPE, which kills a
215	  # non-interactive shell outright - ignore it for the duration of this
216	  # function (localtraps restores the user's disposition on return) so the
217	  # write degrades into the error path instead. The pid pre-check makes the
218	  # common case cheap; the trap covers the race.
219	  setopt localoptions localtraps
220	  trap '' PIPE
221	
222	  # never pass a possibly-zero pid to kill: `kill -0 0` signals the caller's
223	  # own process group and always succeeds
224	  [[ $_omp_serve_pid -gt 0 ]] || return 1
225	  kill -0 $_omp_serve_pid 2>/dev/null || return 1

227	  (( _omp_serve_cycle++ ))
228	  _omp_transient_prompt=""

230	  local name env_json

232	  env_json="\"PATH\":\"$REPLY\""

237	    [[ ${parameters[$name]} == *export* ]] || continue

252	  json+=',"shell":"zsh"'

254	  json+=",\"status\":$_omp_status"

257	  json+=",\"execution-time\":$_omp_execution_time"
258	  json+=",\"stack-count\":$_omp_stack_count"
259	  json+=",\"terminal-width\":${COLUMNS:-0}"
260	  json+=",\"job-count\":$_omp_job_count"
261	  json+=",\"pwd\":\"$REPLY\""
262	  json+=",\"env\":{$env_json}"
263	  json+='}'

265	  print -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_in -- "$json" 2>/dev/null

268	# Renders the primary prompt through the daemon. Returns nonzero on failure,
269	# in which case the caller falls back to the per-prompt stream. The transient
270	# prompt records are cached into $_omp_transient_prompt, either here or by the
271	# async watcher once zle is active.
272	function _omp_serve_render() {
273	  if [[ $_omp_serve_fd_in -lt 0 ]] && ! _omp_serve_start; then
274	    (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
275	    return 1
276	  fi
277	
278	  if ! _omp_serve_request; then
279	    # The daemon died since the last prompt - restart it once.
280	    if ! _omp_serve_start || ! _omp_serve_request; then
281	      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))
282	      _omp_serve_stop
283	      return 1
284	    fi
285	  fi

287	  # Block until this cycle's first primary record; async updates and the
288	  # transient refresh arrive later through the zle watcher. Stale records

290	  local record id payload
291	  while true; do
292	    if ! IFS= read -r -u $_omp_serve_fd_out -d $'\0' -t 2 record; then
293	      (( _omp_serve_failures++ ))

299	    [[ $id == $_omp_serve_cycle ]] || continue
300	    payload=${record#*$'\x1f'}

302	    if [[ $payload == $'\x1e'* ]]; then
303	      _omp_transient_prompt=${payload#$'\x1e'}

307	    _omp_primary_prompt=$payload

381	func copyRecords(id int64, records <-chan string, out *os.File) chan struct{} {
382		done := make(chan struct{})
383	
384		go func() {
385			defer close(done)
386	
387			for record := range records {

389				// atomically with respect to other os.Stdout writers in this

392				// stdout pipe raises SIGPIPE (default disposition on fd 1) and
393				// terminates the daemon - the desired lifecycle when the shell
394				// disappears without sending quit, and the only exit signal in
395				// the request-pipe transport where stdin EOF never arrives.

content.text:
## Analysis of [issue #7660](https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7660)

Both symptoms trace to the zsh **serve daemon** (`coproc oh-my-posh serve`), which ships since v29.22.0 and starts automatically whenever streaming is enabled — the reporter upgraded from a pre-serve version, so this is new to them. I reproduced and verified the mechanics in a real pty (WSL zsh with a clean `zsh -f`).

### Symptom 1: `[4] 15713` on first prompt — confirmed root cause

`_omp_serve_start` launches the daemon with `coproc` ([omp.zsh:170](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:170)). In an interactive zsh with job control (`MONITOR`), `coproc` prints a job-start notice exactly like a `&` background job — I reproduced `[1] 295` under a pty. It fires on the first precmd, which matches "when the prompt is rendered for the first time". The `disown %+` on line 189 comes too late; the notice is printed at spawn. The job number `[4]` just means the reporter's setup had other jobs — immaterial. The per-prompt stream path is not involved: process substitution is not a job and prints nothing.

**Fix (verified, one line):** add `setopt localoptions no_monitor` at the top of `_omp_serve_start`. I tested three things under a pty:

1. With `no_monitor`, the coproc spawns silently — no job line.
2. A stderr redirect around the coproc does **not** suppress the notice, so that alternative is out.
3. Side-effect check: with `no_monitor` the daemon lands in the shell's process group, but POSIX no-job-control semantics make zsh spawn it with SIGINT/SIGQUIT ignored — I sent a pgroup-wide SIGINT and the coproc survived. Go preserves an inherited SIG_IGN for SIGINT (it never installs a handler unless `signal.Notify` is called, and [serve.go](src/cli/serve.go) does no signal handling), so the daemon actually becomes *more* resilient to a stray Ctrl+C, not less. Lifecycle stays governed by stdin EOF / SIGPIPE as designed, and `$!`, `kill -0`, and `disown` all still work.

### Symptom 2: GoLand "Process 'Terminal Local' Is Running" — inherent to the design

JetBrains terminals check the shell's child-process tree when you close a tab and ask for confirmation if anything is alive. The serve daemon is a session-long child of zsh by design, so the dialog will always appear — the `zshexit` handler can't help because the IDE checks *before* terminating the shell. This isn't zsh-specific: any serve-enabled shell (pwsh, fish, cmd) inside a JetBrains IDE will trigger the same dialog.

Options, roughly in order of effort:

- **Document it** — it's a benign confirmation; the daemon dies cleanly on pipe EOF when the shell is killed anyway.
- **Add an opt-out** — there's currently no toggle to disable the serve daemon (only the internal 3-failure fallback), so users bothered by the dialog have no recourse short of disabling streaming entirely. A cheap escape hatch regardless of this issue.
- **Detach the daemon from the process tree** (re-exec with setsid so it reparents to init) — makes JetBrains blind to it, but breaks the script's `$!`/`kill -0` liveness tracking and adds platform-specific complexity. I'd not do this for a cosmetic confirmation dialog.

### Recommendation

Ship the `no_monitor` one-liner for symptom 1 (clear bug, verified fix, no downside found), and treat symptom 2 as a docs/opt-out discussion. Want me to implement the fix and draft a reply to the issue?
Can we not capture the Ctrl+C or exit in a way and kill the daemon prior to the shell exit?
Short answer: we already do — and it doesn't help, because of *when* GoLand performs the check. Let me confirm the JetBrains behavior to be precise.

## Terminal Process Termination Confirmation

To terminate a running session, you close the tab by clicking and confirm the process termination. This is the standard behavior when closing a terminal tab with running processes.

## Where to Find Terminal Settings

Press Ctrl+Alt+S to open settings and then select Tools | Terminal. Alternatively, click on the Terminal toolbar and select Settings.

## Advanced Terminal Settings

For the less frequently used settings, refer to Advanced Settings: Terminal. For advanced settings, select Advanced Settings and scroll down to the Terminal group. For more information about the available settings, refer to Advanced Settings: Terminal.

The specific setting you're looking for (which would control whether to prompt on process termination for child processes) is likely found in the **Advanced Settings** section of your JetBrains IDE under the Terminal group, though the exact setting name isn't explicitly detailed in the current documentation. No — and the reason is ordering, not a missing hook. The daemon is already killed on every real exit path; the GoLand dialog fires *before* any of those paths begin.

What already happens today:

- **Clean exit** (`exit`, Ctrl+D): the `zshexit` handler runs `_omp_serve_quit` ([omp.zsh:363](src/shell/scripts/omp.zsh:363)) — sends the `quit` command and closes the pipes. Daemon gone before the shell process dies.
- **Shell dies uncleanly** (SIGKILL, crash, terminal vanishes): the daemon's stdin hits EOF and it exits; if it's mid-write, SIGPIPE kills it. That's the designed lifecycle in [serve.go:392](src/cli/serve.go:392).
- **Ctrl+C** doesn't exit the shell at all — it aborts the current line or foreground command. There's nothing to capture there, and we explicitly *don't* want the daemon dying on Ctrl+C, since the next prompt needs it.

Why none of this prevents the dialog: when you click the tab's close button, GoLand walks the shell's child-process tree **first**, while the shell is still sitting idle at a prompt. It finds `oh-my-posh serve` alive — which it must be at that moment, that's its whole job — and shows the confirmation. Only after you click "Terminate" does the IDE actually kill the shell, at which point our cleanup (or EOF) takes the daemon down as intended. No trap in the shell can run before a check that happens without anything being sent to the shell.

So the dialog is purely cosmetic — nothing leaks, and confirming it tears everything down correctly. The only technical fix on our side is making the daemon *not a child* of the shell (spawn detached / reparent to init so the IDE's tree walk can't see it), with the costs I mentioned: `$!`-based liveness tracking breaks, and it needs per-platform detach logic. On the user side, JetBrains keeps its terminal confirmation behavior under Settings → Tools → Terminal and the Advanced Settings Terminal group, though I couldn't confirm a documented toggle for this exact dialog.

My take for the issue reply: fix the `[4] 15713` line with the `no_monitor` one-liner, and explain the GoLand dialog as expected behavior for a persistent prompt daemon — same thing users see with any long-running child. If it generates recurring complaints, a detached-spawn mode or a serve opt-out is the follow-up.

Sources: [Terminal settings — IntelliJ IDEA](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/idea/settings-tools-terminal.html), [Terminal — IntelliJ IDEA Documentation](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/idea/terminal-emulator.html)

Okay, you can comment on the issue for me. I agree with the observations, and I agree with only doing the actual fix for the job that gets printed. Do a proper explanation only of the things you know, not anything you cannot actually verify, like which settings are available, etc. Just explain the behavior, why that's happening, and why we can't really fix that on our end because of the way the terminal is quit.  Fix that one issue, create a commit. I'll do the pull request creation.
I'll fix the script, verify it end-to-end in WSL, commit, and then post the issue comment. First, let me pull up my WSL zsh test harness notes and the writing skill for the comment.

# Writing Clearly and Concisely

## Overview

Write with clarity and force. This skill covers what to do (Strunk) and what not to do (AI patterns).

## When to Use This Skill

Use this skill whenever you write content a human will read:

- Emails, messages, and any direct communication
- Documents, reports, summaries, proposals
- Documentation, README files, technical explanations
- Commit messages, pull request descriptions
- Error messages, UI copy, help text, comments
- Any other prose meant for human eyes

**If a human will read it, this skill applies — no exceptions.**

## Limited Context Strategy

When context is tight:

1. Write your draft using judgment
2. Dispatch a subagent with your draft and the relevant section file
3. Have the subagent copyedit and return the revision

Loading a single section (~1,000-4,500 tokens) instead of everything saves significant context.

## Elements of Style

William Strunk Jr.'s *The Elements of Style* (1918) teaches you to write clearly and cut ruthlessly.

### Rules

**Elementary Rules of Usage (Grammar/Punctuation)**:

1. Form possessive singular by adding 's
2. Use comma after each term in series except last
3. Enclose parenthetic expressions between commas
4. Comma before conjunction introducing co-ordinate clause
5. Don't join independent clauses by comma
6. Don't break sentences in two
7. Participial phrase at beginning refers to grammatical subject

**Elementary Principles of Composition**:

8. One paragraph per topic
9. Begin paragraph with topic sentence
10. **Use active voice**
11. **Put statements in positive form**
12. **Use definite, specific, concrete language**
13. **Omit needless words**
14. Avoid succession of loose sentences
15. Express co-ordinate ideas in similar form
16. **Keep related words together**
17. Keep to one tense in summaries
18. **Place emphatic words at end of sentence**

### Reference Files

The rules above are summarized from Strunk's original text. For complete explanations with examples:

| Section | File | ~Tokens |
|---------|------|---------|
| Grammar, punctuation, comma rules | `02-elementary-rules-of-usage.md` | 2,500 |
| Paragraph structure, active voice, concision | `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md` | 4,500 |
| Headings, quotations, formatting | `04-a-few-matters-of-form.md` | 1,000 |
| Word choice, common errors | `05-words-and-expressions-commonly-misused.md` | 4,000 |

**Most tasks need only `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md`** — it covers active voice, positive form, concrete language, and omitting needless words.

## AI Writing Patterns to Avoid

LLMs regress to statistical means, producing generic, puffy prose. Avoid:

- **Puffery:** pivotal, crucial, vital, testament, enduring legacy
- **Empty "-ing" phrases:** ensuring reliability, showcasing features, highlighting capabilities
- **Promotional adjectives:** groundbreaking, seamless, robust, cutting-edge
- **Overused AI vocabulary:** delve, leverage, multifaceted, foster, realm, tapestry
- **Em dashes:** never use em dashes (— or --); restructure the sentence instead
- **Formatting overuse:** excessive bullets, emoji decorations, bold on every other word

Be specific, not grandiose. Say what it actually does.

For comprehensive research on why these patterns occur, see `signs-of-ai-writing.md`. Wikipedia editors developed this guide to detect AI-generated submissions — their patterns are well-documented and field-tested.

## Bottom Line

Writing for humans? Load the relevant section from `elements-of-style/` and apply the rules. For most tasks, `03-elementary-principles-of-composition.md` covers what matters most.

  _omp_serve_stop

  coproc "$_omp_executable" serve --shell=zsh 2>/dev/null

input.command:
gh issue comment 7660 --repo JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Thanks for the detailed report. Both symptoms come from the same feature: with streaming enabled, oh-my-posh keeps a persistent `oh-my-posh serve` process per shell session, which renders prompts in memory instead of spawning a new process for every prompt.

**The `[4] 15713` line**

The zsh integration starts that daemon with `coproc` on the first prompt. In an interactive shell with job control, zsh announces a coproc on the terminal exactly like a background job started with `&`, which is the line you see. We `disown` the daemon right after, but that runs too late: zsh prints the notice at spawn time.

Fixed by starting the coproc with job control disabled for the spawn (`setopt localoptions no_monitor`). The notice is gone, and as a bonus zsh now starts the daemon with SIGINT ignored, so a stray Ctrl+C at the prompt can't take it down. The fix will be in the next release.

**The GoLand dialog**

That one we can't fix on our end. When you close a terminal tab, the IDE checks the shell's child processes before it tells the shell anything. The serve daemon is alive at that point by design, since it has to be running to render your next prompt, so the IDE finds a child process and asks for confirmation. No signal or exit hook reaches the shell before that check, so there is nothing we can intercept.

Confirming the dialog is harmless. The daemon's lifetime is tied to the shell: on a clean exit the shell tells it to quit, and when the shell is killed the daemon exits the moment its pipes close. Nothing keeps running after the tab is gone.
EOF
)"
